topic: Someone finds an unsigned birthday card in their mailbox
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard was thick, slightly softened by the dampness of the mail slot. It was an invitation to a celebration, a brightly colored piece of paper, but the recipient's name was missing. My fingers traced the blank space where a birthday wish should have been, a strange, heavy pressure settling in my chest. I unfolded it slowly, the crisp edges crinkling under my touch. It felt like a misplaced responsibility, an obligation I hadn't been asked to uphold. I tucked it deep into my wallet, the paper feeling alien against my skin. Every time I reached for the mail, a flicker of unease would cross my face, a quick, involuntary tightening around my eyes.

[story 2]

I found it tucked beneath a utility bill, a small, cream-colored card with elegant, looping script on the front, utterly devoid of a sender's name. My breath hitched as I read the perfectly formed sentiment—a warm wish for a happy day—and the realization washed over me like a cold wave. Who had taken the time to write something so thoughtful, only to leave the source completely obscured? I stared at the card, my gaze darting around the quiet living room as if expecting the author to materialize. A nervous tremor ran through my hands as I folded it back up, the movement jerky and hesitant. I couldn't bring myself to discard it; it felt like holding a secret that wasn't mine to keep.

[story 3]

The mail pile was overflowing, a chaotic testament to the day’s deliveries. Then, amidst the junk flyers and bills, something caught my eye—a pristine card, clearly intended for a birthday, yet completely unaddressed. A knot tightened in my stomach, a dull ache spreading through my shoulders. I picked it up, the texture smooth and unfamiliar. My jaw tightened as I scanned the message, trying to assign a voice to the sentiment, but it remained just a beautiful, empty echo. I quickly slid it under a stack of magazines, my movements too sharp, too hurried. I avoided looking at the mailbox for the rest of the afternoon, the silence of the house suddenly amplifying the frantic rhythm of my own pulse.
